title: The practice of saying what you actually think out loud
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
I said yes when I meant no. Again. 

 I was sitting in a coffee shop with a friend. She was telling me about a project she wanted to start. A big one. One...
htmlContent:
I said yes when I meant no. Again.

I was sitting in a coffee shop with a friend. She was telling me about a project she wanted to start. A big one. One that would require a lot of help. And I felt the familiar tightening in my chest. The knot in my stomach. The quiet voice in my head screaming "please don't ask me to help with this." And then she asked. And I said "sure, I'd love to."

My mouth moved before my brain could catch up. Before my body could get a word in edgewise. I walked home that day feeling hollow and exhausted. Not because of the work I'd agreed to do. But because I had betrayed myself again. I had traded my honesty for safety. My truth for approval. My voice for silence.

Here's the thing. Saying what you actually think out loud is one of the most terrifying things a human being can do. Especially if you grew up in an environment where your thoughts didn't matter. Where your feelings were dismissed. Where speaking up meant getting hurt.

I've spent years studying this. Reading the research. Working with my own therapist. And I keep coming back to the same uncomfortable truth. The people who heal the most are the ones who start saying the unsayable. Out loud. To someone. Anyone. Even if it's just to themselves in the mirror.

This isn't about being rude or cruel. It's not about "speaking your truth" like some kind of weapon. It's about the quiet, terrifying act of letting the outside world know what's actually happening inside you. Right now. In this moment.

The nervous system doesn't lie

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to safety and threat. Here's what he found. Your body is constantly scanning for cues of danger or safety. It does this without your permission. Without your conscious input. It's happening right now as you read these words.

When you're about to say something you actually think, your nervous system makes a split-second decision. Is it safe to speak? Or is it safer to stay quiet? And if you grew up in a home where honesty was punished, where your true thoughts were met with anger or shame or withdrawal of love, your nervous system learned one thing. Silence equals survival.

Think about it. Every time you swallow your words, your body registers that as a win. You stayed safe. You avoided conflict. You kept the connection intact. But here's the cost. Each swallowed word is a small death. A tiny betrayal that accumulates over years until you barely recognize yourself anymore.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office three years ago. She asked me what I was feeling. And I opened my mouth to say "I'm fine." The words were right there. Ready. Automatic. But something stopped me. Maybe it was the way she was looking at me. Maybe it was the exhaustion of pretending for thirty years. I don't know. But instead of "I'm fine," I said "I'm terrified."

And nothing bad happened. The room didn't explode. She didn't leave. She didn't tell me I was too much. She just nodded and said "tell me more."

That moment changed something in my nervous system. It was a tiny crack of light in a very dark room. But it was enough to start believing that maybe, just maybe, honesty wouldn't kill me.

The cost of the performance

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown us that trauma isn't just a story in your head. It's a physical reality in your body. When you spend years performing a version of yourself that isn't real, your body pays the price. Chronic pain. Autoimmune issues. Digestive problems. Fatigue. The body keeps track of every lie you've told to keep yourself safe.

I used to think I was just a people-pleaser. A nice person. Someone who was easy to get along with. But the truth is darker than that. I was a survival artist. I had learned to read every room, every face, every subtle shift in tone, and adjust myself accordingly. I was a chameleon. And I was exhausted.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did you feel a familiar heaviness in your chest? That's the cost of the performance. It lives in your body. And it doesn't go away until you stop performing.

The practice of saying what you actually think out loud is not a one-time event. It's not like you decide to be honest and suddenly you're free. It's a practice. A daily, hourly, sometimes minute-by-minute practice of noticing when you're about to say the safe thing, and choosing to say the real thing instead.

Start small. The next time someone asks how you are, don't say "fine." Say "honestly, I'm struggling today." The next time someone asks for your opinion, don't say "whatever you think." Say "I actually disagree, and here's why." The next time someone asks you to do something you don't want to do, don't say "sure." Say "I can't do that right now."

Wild, right? The idea that you could just say what's true and the world wouldn't end. I'm not saying it will be easy. I'm saying it's worth it. Because the alternative is a life lived in the prison of your own silence.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). It walks you through the actual mechanics of saying no, of stating your needs, of letting people know where you end and they begin. It's not theoretical. It's practical. And for someone who has spent their whole life saying yes when they meant no, it can be a lifeline.

The grief of being seen

Here's something nobody tells you about saying what you actually think. People will leave. Some relationships cannot survive your honesty. And that's devastating. But it's also information.

When I started telling people what I actually thought, I lost friends. I lost the approval of family members. I lost the comfortable role of "the easy one" in my relationships. And I grieved. Hard. I questioned whether I was doing the right thing. Whether I was being selfish. Whether I was ruining everything.

But here's what I also found. The relationships that survived were deeper. More real. More alive. The people who stayed were the ones who actually wanted to know me, not the performance. And the ones who left? They were never really in a relationship with me anyway. They were in a relationship with the character I was playing.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who studied under Bessel van der Kolk, talks about the concept of "parts" in trauma survivors. We all have different parts of ourselves that developed to handle different situations. The part that keeps you quiet to stay safe. The part that criticizes you for being too much. The part that desperately wants to be seen and heard. These parts aren't bad. They were trying to protect you. But they need to be updated. They need to know that you're not in that dangerous childhood home anymore. That you're an adult now. That you can handle the consequences of being honest.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. It names the experience of growing up in environments where you couldn't be yourself. It gives language to the shame, the fear, the constant vigilance. And it offers a path forward. A way to start telling the truth about your past so you can start telling the truth about your present.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why I was so afraid to speak my mind. Why I felt responsible for everyone else's feelings. Why honesty felt like a threat to my survival. Reading it was like someone finally turned on the lights in a room I'd been stumbling around in for decades.

The practice itself

So how do you actually do this? How do you start saying what you think when your whole system is screaming at you to stay quiet?

You start with your body. Before you speak, pause. Take a breath. Notice what's happening in your chest, your throat, your stomach. That tightness you feel? That's not weakness. That's your nervous system trying to protect you. Thank it. And then speak anyway.

You start with small truths. "I don't want to go to that party." "I'm feeling overwhelmed right now." "I need a minute." These aren't big confessions. They're tiny acts of rebellion against the habit of hiding.

You practice in s. With a therapist. A trusted friend. A support group. You say the thing you've been holding in. And you let yourself feel what happens when nobody attacks you for it. You let your nervous system learn a new pattern. Honesty is safe. At least sometimes. At least with some people.

You practice with yourself. Say it out loud when you're alone. "I'm angry." "I'm sad." "I'm scared." "I don't know what I want." Let the words exist in the air. Let them be real. Let them have weight.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down Polyvagal Theory into something you can actually use. Exercises. Practices. Ways to help your nervous system feel safe enough to let your real voice come through.

I'm not going to tell you this is easy. It's not. Some days it feels impossible. Some days the old patterns are so strong that you'll say the safe thing before you even realize what happened. That's okay. That's not failure. That's practice.

Here's what I know. Every time you say what you actually think, you're building a new neural pathway. You're teaching your nervous system that honesty is survivable. You're showing yourself that you can handle the consequences of being real. And slowly, over time, the gap between what you think and what you say starts to close.

You don't have to do this all at once. You don't have to tell everyone everything. You just have to start. One truth. One moment. One breath. One word at a time.

And maybe, eventually, you'll find yourself in a coffee shop with a friend, and she'll ask you for something you can't give, and you'll hear yourself say "I can't do that." And you'll feel the fear. And you'll feel the old pull to take it back, to smooth it over, to make yourself small again. But you won't. Because you'll know, deep in your body, that the truth is worth the discomfort. That being seen is worth the risk. That you are worth the honesty.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Anchored (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
